title: Creating Your First Table
intro: Time to create your own database table from scratch! You're building a bookstore app and need a table to store books. We'll use SQLite to create the table, insert some data, and query it.

teach.title: CREATE TABLE = Designing a Form
teach.body: Creating a table is like designing a paper form before printing hundreds of copies. You decide what fields to include (name, author, price), what format each field uses (text, number, date), and which fields are required. Once the form is designed, everyone fills in the same structure.

teach.title: The Blueprint Before Building
teach.body: Creating a table is like drawing the blueprint for a house before pouring any concrete. You decide how many rooms (columns), what each room is for (data types), and which rooms are mandatory (NOT NULL). If you skip the blueprint and start building randomly, you end up with doors that lead to walls. A good CREATE TABLE statement is your blueprint -- plan it once, and every record that moves in fits perfectly.

teach.title: The CREATE TABLE Command
teach.body:
The basic pattern is:

```
CREATE TABLE table_name (
  column_name DATA_TYPE CONSTRAINTS,
  column_name DATA_TYPE CONSTRAINTS
);
```

You define each column with a name, a data type, and optional constraints like NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY, UNIQUE, or DEFAULT.

teach.title: A Complete Table Definition
teach.code:
CREATE TABLE books (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  title TEXT NOT NULL,
  author TEXT NOT NULL,
  price DECIMAL(8, 2) NOT NULL,
  pages INTEGER,
  published_year INTEGER,
  in_stock BOOLEAN DEFAULT true
);
teach.annotation: AUTOINCREMENT makes the id count up automatically (1, 2, 3...). title, author, and price are required (NOT NULL). pages and published_year are optional. in_stock defaults to true.

teach.body: Creating tables is the first real step in building a production app. When you vibecode a SaaS product, the AI needs you to describe your tables. Nail this skill and you can spin up apps with proper data storage that are ready for paying users from day one.

CREATE TABLE defines a new table with columns, data types, and constraints. Always include a PRIMARY KEY, mark essential columns as NOT NULL, and use DEFAULT for columns with sensible automatic values.

steps.instruction: Let's create a new SQLite database for our bookstore. Open a new database file called bookstore.db.
steps.output:
SQLite version 3.42.0
Enter ".help" for usage hints.
sqlite>
steps.hint: Type 'sqlite3 bookstore.db' to create and open the database.

steps.instruction: Now create a 'books' table with columns: id (integer primary key), title (text, required), author (text, required), and price (decimal, required). Use AUTOINCREMENT for the id.
steps.expectedCommand: CREATE TABLE books (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, title TEXT NOT NULL, author TEXT NOT NULL, price DECIMAL(8,2) NOT NULL);
acceptAlternatives: CREATE TABLE books (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, title TEXT NOT NULL, author TEXT NOT NULL, price DECIMAL(8, 2) NOT NULL);
CREATE TABLE books(id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,title TEXT NOT NULL,author TEXT NOT NULL,price DECIMAL(8,2) NOT NULL);
steps.hint: Use CREATE TABLE books (...); with the columns defined inside the parentheses.

steps.instruction: The table was created. Let's insert a book. Add 'The Pragmatic Programmer' by 'David Thomas' priced at 44.99.
steps.expectedCommand: INSERT INTO books (title, author, price) VALUES ('The Pragmatic Programmer', 'David Thomas', 44.99);
acceptAlternatives: INSERT INTO books(title, author, price) VALUES('The Pragmatic Programmer', 'David Thomas', 44.99);
steps.hint: Use INSERT INTO books (title, author, price) VALUES ('...', '...', ...);

steps.instruction: Now let's verify the book was inserted. Select all records from the books table.
steps.hint: Use SELECT * FROM books; to see all rows.

exercise.completionMessage: You just created a database table from scratch, inserted a record, and queried it back! Notice how the id was automatically set to 1 -- that's AUTOINCREMENT doing its job. The title, author, and price columns are all NOT NULL, so the database will reject any book without those fields. This is the foundation of database design: define your structure first, then fill it with data.
